segments.text: In this episode, you'll learn a lot more about how ARK and Kalshi have partnered and how maybe
this is the start of a major new financial innovation wave.
Greetings, everyone. My name is Kathy Wood, CEO and CIO of ARK Invest. And we have a very special
in-the-know today. No, it's not Employment Friday, but it is with great pleasure that we announce
our partnership with Kalshi. And with me here today is the person who made it happen,
Nick Gruss, our Director of Research for Consumer AI and FinTech. Welcome, Nick.
Thank you, Kathy.
So, Nick, why don't you, since you have been critical to establishing this partnership,
why don't you set it up and give our viewers a sense of what they're going to see today?
Yeah, thank you so much, Kathy. And thank you for having me on. I think this partnership is years,
months in the making. If you think about when we really started first looking at prediction markets
for data insights, this goes back a few years now. But it really is months in the making. We've
been talking with the Kalshi team because what we realized was we're looking at all of these markets
that Kalshi and other platforms have created. But what we realized is that there is a certain level
of insight that we have looking at the technology platforms that we cover, the companies we cover,
the macroeconomic statistics that you go over in this show, realizing that some of the markets we
would like to see actually had not been created. And so we actually reached out to Kalshi and said,
hey, we think that this could be a great opportunity to collaborate, to share ideas,
to surface data and insights that we think others will also find valuable and create markets around
those and then be able to surface the wisdom of the crowd. Right now, we're not participating in
these markets. We're using them as data insights. So we're not trading in them. We're looking at
them as data insights because we want to hear what you, the listener and others around the world
think about some of the opportunities we're looking at. So that's what we've done with Kalshi.
You'll see a number of those markets today. There's also some markets you won't see today
because they're more focused in the genomics space. There's a new medicine tab now on Kalshi
and there's going to be plenty more markets listed that cover all different sorts of
interesting, innovative technologies going forward. This is an ongoing partnership that we're really
excited about. Yeah. And Nick, as we were discussing this and have been discussing this for
several months, it occurred to me in speaking with Kalshi that, wait a minute, this movement
is the beginnings, I think, of bringing active equity management back into style
because we're talking about events that should move stocks. And if we have more people looking
for and at those events, they're going to be interested in investing in some of these
opportunities and maybe taking a little bit out of that passive portfolio that is
tied to the broader based indexes. So I'm pretty excited about this. This could be
the beginning of a resurgence in active equity management. Yeah, absolutely. And I think when
we've talked about it, prediction markets really represent the purest form of risk out there because
as you point out, Cathy, you can go directly to the underlying discrete event that then moves the
on an equity. And so I actually think of these tools as almost the inverse of derivatives,
right? It's the underlying catalyst that will drive the performance of a stock up or down.
And so whereas you may want to hedge out some of that risk using a put call spread or many other
forms of hedging, now you have direct access to those underlying events, business KPIs,
macroeconomic events where you don't need to take on the extra risk that is associated with
the market, right? If you don't want to be exposed to what is happening in Iran,
well, you don't need to take on that exposure through derivatives, which are going to
inherently have some of that in it. You can go directly to the underlying event.
So I think it's a really amazing opportunity for the financial markets when we've started
doing this research. And I'll give a sneak preview, I think, you know, in the medium term.
So over the next few years, we believe that this market could explode up to five trillion dollars
in notional volume. Now, right now, Cal, she is on an annualized run rate to do around one hundred
and twenty one hundred and thirty billion dollars in notional volume for this year.
So we're talking about a massive explosion of where this total market is headed.
But when you put it into the perspective of the total derivatives market, which in 2024
was around seven hundred and fifteen trillion dollars in notional volume,
you're only talking about 70 basis points, right? That five five trillion dollar figure over 715.
So it's a drop in the bucket compared to where traditional derivatives sit today.
And as you point out, I think this is an expansion of the addressable market that derivatives
don't necessarily give you today in terms of the risk profile.
Yeah, pretty exciting. So innovation in the financial world. Love it. We're going to go
through some of these charts. We'll do a lot of our normal in the no drill, but we've got some
Kelsey Kelsey charts to accompany some some of the charts we normally show you. So here we go.
OK, so let's let's start with our first Kelsey slide. And here you can see in the prediction
markets. One of the questions we put in is, will the U.S. federal deficit to GDP ratio drop below
5 percent for fiscal year 2026? Now, fiscal year means through the end of September. So
the year through September of 26. And as you can see here, as the war in the Middle East has
extended, the odds that the GDP, the debt deficit to GDP ratio drops below 5 percent.
Those have dropped, understandably. So let's take a look at where we are. So here is the
GDP. It's at about 5.12 percent. And you can see it was moving in the right direction
before the war. And we really thought it was going to drop below 5 percent and head for that 3
percent, which is Treasury Secretary Besant's goal for the end of 28. In fact, we thought
that it was going to drop below that 3 percent well before before the end of 28.
Well, the war has changed that. Now, the defense budget is going up and it was already at about
a trillion dollars. And just to put this in in perspective, the deficit is is at about
well, the total government spending is at about seven trillion dollars. And so one of that is
defense. Two of that is health care. And one of the downward pressures on the budget is health
care. We've heard about Medicare actually cutting prices dramatically in terms of some of the
most heavily used drugs out there. We're also seeing Doge continue to cut back on federal
employment. And yet we do have this war. So the odds have gone down, certainly have
in the short term. But if we if we get past this period into fiscal 27, we think we'll have
another shot at getting below 5 percent and heading actually very quickly to 3 percent.
And the reason for that is we see GDP growing so much more quickly than many economists expect.
But very short term, the odds the odds have dropped. Here's another reason the odds have
but this is a good news reason I've mentioned on.
on past in the nose, the accelerated depreciation that was in the tax package last year.
And you can see the impact already that it's having.
So corporate tax receipts are going to be, I think because of the accelerated depreciation,
roughly $150 billion lower.
Now obviously that's good news for the economy.
It's one reason that we expect GDP growth to accelerate fairly dramatically in the not
too distant future.
And here, and we've presented these deficits together, deficit, I just showed you the federal
deficit as a percent of GDP.
Here's the other deficit that bothers, well, especially President Trump.
I don't think it bothers too many other people, but his objective is to move us into surplus.
And you can see we have spent many, many years in a deficit position.
And from our point of view, we'll probably continue to spend more years in deficit.
Why is that?
It is because we think with the stimulative fiscal policy, especially that tax package,
that the U.S. economy is going to boom relative to other economies.
So we're going to be importing more than we export.
Now the other side of this, which takes away the sting that I think President Trump thinks
exists, is a capital surplus.
It means more investment dollars coming into the United States relative to our dollars
going elsewhere in the world.
So a capital surplus.
Here is another take, and I'm going to credit Don Lustwin from Trend Macro for this chart.
And what he does, trying to help people understand the debt and the deficit situation, is he
says, you know, when you compare debt to GDP, what you're comparing is a balance sheet item
to an income statement item, the equivalent of.
Really, you should be comparing debt to equity.
So equity is the black line, debt is the purple, and GDP is the red.
And if we go to the next chart, you will see that while debt to GDP is above one here,
the one ratio, you can see debt to equity is at 0.5.
And actually it's very low by historical standards.
The other thing I will say is if you look at debt to assets, another balance sheet item,
the asset side of the U.S. balance sheet is really at cost.
What did the land cost?
What did the gold cost when it went onto the federal balance sheet?
Well, some of these costs were 50 to 75 years ago.
And so if we really thought about this, we could sell some of our land, and I forget
how much land the government owns.
It's a substantial, I think it's over a quarter of all the land in the United States.
So we could defray the debt that way.
Now, I'm not recommending it, and we do want discipline.
So I'm very happy that there is a concern about the deficit and the debt, because if
you think about government spending, which is the problem, every incremental dollar in
government spending is effectively an incremental tax dollar.
We've either got to pay for it today in taxes, tomorrow in taxes, or the most regressive
tax of all is inflated away, which we don't think that's going to happen, but that is
what happened during the 70s.
So back to Calixte, another hotly debated issue is the dollar.
DXY is the trade-weighted dollar.
It's only six countries.
It's not the 26 countries in the Fed's measure.
It doesn't include China, the yuan, for example.
But how high will the dollar get?
Last year, the narrative was the U.S. exceptionalism is dead, the dollar is in decline, and it's
inexorable.
It's going to continue.
And you can see here, many people believe that the dollar will end the year, as measured
by the DXY, at 1036.
Now, that is very interesting to us because you can see on the next page where we are
in the context of history.
We are just below 99.
So what Calixte is suggesting is the people who are willing to trade or make a prediction,
they actually think the dollar is going to go up.
And that goes against conventional wisdom.
Now we agree that it is going to go up, mostly because of what I just mentioned about foreign
direct investment and our capital-friendly, business-friendly tax policies.
Also, deregulation is, we think, going to increase the return on investment in the United
States relative to that in the rest of the world.
That is dollar positive.
And we take this chart all the way back into the early 80s.
Why?
It is because we like to illustrate what happened during Reaganomics.
And we think Trumponomics resembles greatly, actually, Reaganomics.
And look what happened to the dollar back then.
Now it was coming from the depths of despair in the late 70s.
But it really went into overdrive to the point where all the treasury and monetary ministers
around the world got together and said, this is becoming a deflationary risk to the rest
of the world.
Because a lot of debt is denominated, was and still is denominated in dollars.
So the debt service was getting more and more expensive back then.
We think we're not going to see a surge like we did in the early 80s.
But we think the pressure is to the upside.
And if you look at this chart in the context of history, you will see that we are closer,
if you take out that boom in the early 80s, we're closer to the higher end of the dollar's
range than we are to the lower end.
And again, I think that goes against conventional thinking right now.
Here we're moving into monetary policy and away from fiscal policy.
And you can see money growth is up to roughly 5%.
So it has continued to accelerate it, having spent some time in negative territory after
the uncontrolled money growth post-COVID.
You can see that is mapped in green against the CPI.
And this is important.
It's setting us up for another Calshi chart.
And you can see that there is a loose connection between these two metrics.
Part of the reason there's a loose connection is velocity.
Sometimes when the velocity of money moves down, that takes the stimulus out of money
growth because the rate at which money is turning is slowing down.
And that typically happens when people are afraid, concerned, or feeling somewhat negative
about their circumstances.
So, you can see here that velocity actually has been in a downtrend since the late 90s.
And that is when baby boomers started to retire.
Those were the very early baby boomers.
But also, it was around the time that China entered the WTO.
And there was a lot of concern in the United States.
Our manufacturing sector effectively was exported to China.
And so, there were concerns.
And I think those two variables caused the secular decline.
Well, baby boomers are still retiring.
And again, when they retire, they're not as free spending.
And we are now seeing, however, the reversal of the shipment of our manufacturing sector over to China.
And I think our tax package is one reason for that.
So, maybe we're seeing a secular turn in velocity.
If not, it will probably flatline here.
And, you know, because baby boomers are retiring, but the manufacturing is coming back and other kinds of investment coming back.
Maybe we flatline here.
And that 5% money growth is what is impacting directly the economy.
And if you think about 5% money growth, that would effectively mean nominal GDP growth would be around 5%.
And that would give us room.
We think real growth, thanks to productivity, is going to accelerate into the 3%, 4%, 5% range.
That would leave very little room for inflation.
So, here's another look at monetary policy from a very short-term point of view,
meaning it's the yield curve as measured by the two-year treasury yield minus the three-month treasury yield.
And in negative territory, that would suggest the Fed was leaning, I would say, hawkish or restrictive.
And you can see we've just crossed into a positive slope, just crossed.
So, maybe the Fed is operating at a neutral rate.
It's not too hawkish.
It's not too dovish.
We're in a neutral zone right now.
And this chart I've featured a number of times.
I find it fascinating.
This is the yield curve again, but it's the 10-year treasury yield minus the two-year treasury yield.
And this is the one most commonly referenced.
You can see the downtrend that's been in place really since the 2008-09 debacle, the global financial crisis.
And the reason I'm interested is even after COVID, when monetary authorities around the world were, you know,
put the accelerator to the floor and fiscal policy was profligate.
I mean, just money thrown at the problem around the world.
We couldn't get this measure of the yield curve back into that 200 to 300 basis point range.
And I just have been wondering, what does that mean?
Effectively, it means that the long-term treasury yield was not following short rates the way it normally does.
And why would that be?
And here again, have we hit another peak?
And are we tipping over again?
I don't know.
But what that would tell us, and of course, consider the source, it would fit in with our sort of deflationary undercurrent thesis around technology.
And we saw this in the 50 years that ended in the late 20s, the late 20s in the 1900s, which was also ripe with innovation.
So telephone, electricity, internal combustion engine, all riding on top of the railroads that were built out in the 1800s.
Well, the equivalent to the railroads this time is the internet in the digital world.
And now we have robotics, energy storage, AI, very importantly AI, blockchain technology and multiomics technology.
All of those are highly deflationary trends.
And could the yield curve be picking up on that?
We think it might.
Now, here's productivity, which is a part of that story.
The technology, the innovation platforms that I just mentioned involve 15 different technologies.
They're converging.
And we think that productivity growth is going to soar in the next few years.
So here we ask the question, will non-farm productivity increase above 3% in any quarter?
This is on a year-over-year basis, in any quarter in 26.
And you can see there's a 42% probability.
Well, we think the probability actually is much higher than that.
And it's actually for a statistical reason.
If you look at productivity today, you can see we're at roughly 2.5%.
And you can also see, again, if you look across this chart, this is in the higher half of the history of this chart.
So pretty strong.
And as we have said in the know many times, we've been in a rolling recession and this is what has happened.
That's so interesting.
Housing has been down and out.
Manufacturing has been down and out.
Small business down and out.
Consumers have been in a funk, as measured by sentiment.
And productivity is at 2.5%.
So we believe that these technologies are already having an impact on productivity.
And we think in the first quarter, when the numbers are reported, and they'll be revised two times, I think.
But when the first quarter number is reported, if it is up at all on a quarter to quarter basis,
so from the fourth quarter to the first quarter, because last year's first quarter was down
roughly 0.9% on a quarter to quarter basis, if it's up at all,
now that's annualized.
If it's up at all in the first quarter, and we think it will be on a quarter to quarter basis,
that will take the year over year above 3%.
Now, I know why it's probably at a low 42%.
It's because people are looking at some very big productivity numbers in the second and third quarter of last year.
And if we're below those numbers, then this will come back down.
Nonetheless, that first quarter might actually be above 3%.
And here's unit labor costs, 2.3%, 2.4% on a year over year basis.
And so for those of you worried about inflation, you can see this looks very different from post-COVID.
Unit labor costs soared.
And one of the reasons is wage growth is very well-behaved, very well-behaved.
It was not after COVID as companies were scrambling to find employees so that they could survive,
the companies could survive, and the employees actually.
That's not the case right now.
In fact, a lot of employment indicators suggest we're in a weak environment.
And part of it is AI.
Entry-level jobs are not there for college graduates the way they have been in the past.
So unit labor costs, this is underlying inflation, 2.4%.
What's interesting about that is if you look at the TIPS market,
that's
what the TIPS market says inflation is going to be, in other words, no real improvement from here.
If we're right and productivity starts scaling three and into next year four or five percent,
this unit labor cost growth rate will come down and underlying inflation will come down.
Here are some other reasons inflation is coming down. Again, very different from post-COVID,
when we had that supply shock and many people are saying we're in another supply shock
and we are when it comes to oil for sure, but we are not in a broad-based supply shock
like we were after COVID. Nothing like it. In fact, quite the opposite in housing.
Housing inflation is nil for existing home prices and still negative for new home prices.
And yet here you do see the supply shock when it comes to oil. How long will this last?
Well, the longer the Middle East turmoil continues, the longer this supply shock will last.
And the question is, who's going to pay for it? Is it going to get through to the consumer price
level or is it going to be eaten by the consumer companies who have to pay up but can't get their
pricing through to the end consumer? And we have a strong point of view about that.
Here's another chart. This is commodity, broad-based commodity index. And you can see
a bit of a supply shock, but I would submit that it is more than that. It is demand going up
relative to supply. And this is a call for action, a call for more supply. Again, who's going to pay
for this is the question. Is it going to get through to the consumer price level? Now, here
is the Cal-She betting on that. This is the PPI year-over-year percent change minus the CPI
year-over-year percent change. Is it going to be – is the PPI inflation rate going to be above
the CPI inflation rate for three consecutive months in 2026? So you can see here that the
market is pretty sure that producer price inflation is going to surpass consumer price
inflation on a year-over-year basis for three consecutive months this year. Now, that's very
interesting. Now, what is this saying? Well, you can see it started out closer to 25 or 20 percent
probability, moved to 30 as the initial stages of the war in Iran took off. And then everyone
thought it was going to be over quickly, settled back, and you can see what has happened since.
This is more extended than many observers predicted. And so that's where there's
certainty so far as you can see on the next chart. What's interesting about this is we already have
two months where the PPI inflation rate has surpassed the CPI inflation rate, two consecutive
months. Last – in February, the PPI was 3.4 percent at a year-over-year rate and the CPI 2.4.
And so this, when the PPI is released on April 14th, this contract will probably close out and
we'll probably roll into another one, maybe six consecutive months or something like that. We
shall see. But let me show you – yes, I've shown you the total PPI and CPI, but here on this chart
is the core CPI minus the core PPI. And you can see that even really – or maybe it is in
conjunction with the war – a lot of the producer prices, other than food and energy, have been
increasing at an accelerated rate while the CPI, which you see there in purple, has been
decelerating. That's interesting. That's really interesting. What does this say? It's saying,
perhaps, that consumer goods companies are not able to pass the cost increases along and that
their margins probably will be squeezed. And here you can see that that is true according to
trueflation. So, as measured by trueflation, the year-over-year price consumer inflation rate is
about 1.7 percent. Now, it got as low, I think, as 1.4 or 1.3, has come up a little bit here,
but has not gone anywhere near that 3.4 percent PPI inflation rate.
Even though gasoline prices are a big psychological and impactful category,
other prices must either be coming down or – I find it difficult to explain. And
even more so when we look at the core trueflation inflation rate. It's at 1.1 percent. And you can
see it struggled in the 2 to 3 percent range for two and a half years or so. It has resolved to
the downside. And if you look at what's going on right now, we haven't seen this kind of CPI
core inflation rate since COVID when we were in dire straits. So, that is very interesting.
And here you can see one of the things that's going on with the PPI. The PPI is reflecting
what's going on in the manufacturing sector, to be sure. This is the Purchasing Managers Index,
and there's a clear pickup. Now, I think that this is related to our tax package,
as I explained before. And some of the stimulus is getting into our economy, stimulating the
supply side of the economy, which will be good longer run for the overall inflation rate.
Manufacturers are responding to those higher prices, and they are producing more. Here you
can see this is an outright boom. It's an indicator that I believe that Piper Sandler
gave us or produced. And you can see it is flat. This is U.S. Flatbed Truck Market Demand Index.
So, this is an indicator that something's booming out there. And no one's using the word boom,
but I think there is a call to action. The pricing certainly is one reason. But I also
think there's a lot more foreign direct investment coming into the United States.
Some of it might be tariff related. Some of it is because deregulation and tax policy
are so friendly. So, that's interesting. You can see on the services sector, both orders and,
unfortunately, prices. Prices, again, I think being impacted by what's going on in the Middle
East, and it may be opportunistic pricing around that. Normally, when we see new orders pick up,
the prices are coming down. And I do think that the purple chart will resolve to the downside
once the Middle East turmoil clears up to some extent. Here is why the pricing is not getting
through. Sentiment is terrible, as measured by the University of Michigan. And these are
the three income levels, low, mid, and high. The lower income level in the last month
dropped back to its recent low. So, affordability, unfortunately, still a massive problem.
could really impact the midterm elections. Unemployment. Here's confidence
in as measured by jobs easy to get minus jobs hard to get. Confidence is
deteriorating here. Now a bright spot is that the entry-level jobs, remember that
unemployment rate went before revisions up to 11 percent. It's now
reversing to eight and a half percent. And maybe what's going on there is just
like ARC is doing. You know we're a pretty lean machine and at one point and
I said you know I don't think we'll be hiring people because AI is making us
all so much more productive. But on the business side especially we are we need
to hire more people because what is the refrain I'm hearing from our employee
base. It is you know I know all these tools are out there but I don't have any
time to experiment. I have too much on my plate and so we're bringing in younger
people with one foot through the AI door and very eager to introduce us to new
productivity enhancing ideas. Maybe that's what's going on a little bit more
broadly here I don't know. Here you can see another stress point saving rate
down it dropped to four percent in today's report as income was very weak
but spending again mostly prices was lifted and so a low saving rate is it
typically takes place when consumers have no choice and I think that's where
we are right now or when there's an incredible amount of optimism out there
and more and more people are willing to live on the edge a bit more. We think
it's more the former than the latter. Here you can see an indicator delinquency
rates on autos all-time highs for subprime and and even close to all-time
highs for the overall. Here you can see existing home sales. This is not a
is leading the way and and we think it is. Of course the recent backup in
interest rates does not help this. We also see builders trying to clear clear
their lots of houses. These are new one-family houses for sale. Those are
starting to come down. They're cutting prices to move them and then
here are a few market indicators. We've spent a long time on in the know so
we'll just quickly go through them. We're seeing the S&P to gold and we've
been watching this carefully. Remember it was it was a leading indicator for some
very bad times in the 70s and there are some strategists out there who think
we're going back into those bad times. We do not but this is uncomfortable in that
it has tipped down. This is the offset to some extent and until very recently it's
S&P to crude oil and that uptrend is still in place even with the really near
more than 50% increase in oil prices. Here's Bitcoin to gold. Bitcoin is
starting to outperform gold again. Gold peaked January 28th, the day that Warsh
was announced as the next Fed chairman if Congress approves him. And he is
slightly hawkish. So Bitcoin has been moving up relative to gold. That's
interesting. And then just the last two charts, two or three charts here. This one
is still a puzzle. Why is that green line, metals to gold, still going down? Is it
something really wrong in China? I don't know if it's just China but it is
puzzling especially now that the gold price has gone down from nearly, I think
it was almost, was it $5,600 down to $4,400. We haven't seen a clear turn in
this variable which is quite interesting. It used to correlate very well with the
10-year treasury yield which you see in purple there. They parted ways in 22
when the Fed started tightening and I would say when central banks started
selling gold, I mean buying gold and selling dollars. And again the yen carry
trade unwinding starting in the summer of 24. So all of those should be in
there now and my guess is that the 10-year treasury yield is probably going
to fall because we've had a lot of major forces pushing it up, not the least of
which has been the recent flare-up in commodity prices. If we're right and
supply, the supply side of the economy is coming to the rescue, we're probably
going to be surprised at interest rates coming down as opposed to going up. And
a final chart here, final two charts here, credit default swaps on banks, you
know, private credit, is this infecting, is it going to be a systemic issue? This
chart would suggest no, it's not backing up into the banking system. And this is
the high yield chart, so junk bonds relative to treasuries. They're not
seeing anything on the horizon bothersome. In fact, they're near all-time
lows. So these charts, these last two charts, would say nothing to worry about.
We know there's a lot to worry about. The market has gone through a bout of
volatility here. But if we're right that there's a boom, that inflation is going
to resolve to the downside after this conflict passes, and that productivity,
profitability is going to increase, we think that's very positive for the
equity markets. And I will say one last thing, profit measures came out for the
fourth quarter, and they look very good. Now, again, active management is going
to be important here. Is margin compression going to be a problem for
consumer goods and related companies? In the short term, we think it is. Longer
term, we think it will be another reason for companies to harness AI and other
productivity tools to stem decline in margins, and maybe even increase margins
in the future. So with that, thank you very much. I know this has been a long
one. We wanted to introduce Calshi. We're excited about that partnership, and we're
really excited about the notion that these prediction markets could
bring active equity management back into style. And which group would that help
the most? We think, and we have a lot of questions in the multi-omic sphere to
put into prediction markets, to help educate people about the
unbelievable opportunities in the space, and educate them about how inefficiently
valued that part of the market is. So with that, until next month, I will say
goodbye and wish you a good month.
